title: Foul medames
subtitle: Egyptian slow-cooked broad beans with lemon, garlic, and cumin.
excerpt: The breakfast of Egypt, eaten across the Levant. Broad beans simmered long, then crushed with lemon, garlic, ground cumin, and olive oil into a coarse warm dip. Eaten with flatbread, hard-boiled egg, and pickles. Twenty minutes of work if you start from tinned beans, twelve hours if you start dry.
sourceNotes:
Egyptian home cookery, where foul medames is the canonical breakfast, eaten by street vendors and home kitchens across the country. Documented in twentieth-century Arabic and Egyptian cookbooks; the dish appears in earlier Arabic culinary texts as far back as the medieval period.

The brown fava bean (foul) is the proper bean. The smaller, mottled fava bean used in Egypt holds its shape better than the larger fresh broad beans you find in European spring markets. Most foreign cooks use tinned brown fava beans; they are sold in Middle Eastern shops.

Variants vary the toppings; the bean base is the same.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freezes well for up to two months once the beans are cooked but before the toppings go on. Defrost overnight in the fridge, reheat gently with a splash of water, then add lemon and toppings fresh.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles cleanly. A wide pan helps the beans cook evenly.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Cook the beans the day before; the flavour deepens overnight. Reheat gently with a splash of water; the lemon and oil go on fresh.

recipeTools.notes: A potato masher gives the right rough texture. A fork works for smaller batches.

glossaryTerms.definition: The small brown fava bean used across Egypt and the Levant for foul medames. Holds its shape on long cooking better than the larger fresh broad beans of European spring markets. Tinned brown fava beans, sold in Middle Eastern shops, are the convenient stand-in.

content.text: Foul medames is the breakfast of Egypt and one of the older dishes of the Arab world. Brown fava beans simmered slowly, then crushed with garlic, lemon, ground cumin, and olive oil until the texture is somewhere between dip and stew. Warm, brown, savoury. Eaten with flatbread, a hard-boiled egg, pickles, and a glass of sweet tea.
The proper bean is the small brown
foul bean

, sold dried or tinned in Middle Eastern shops. Tinned brown fava beans are the everyday stand-in; the cooked bean is already soft, and twenty minutes on the hob pulls the dish together. Dried beans give a slightly cleaner flavour but want overnight soaking and an hour and a half on the hob.

The recipe is austere by design. The flavour comes from cumin, lemon, garlic, and oil; not from a long list of ingredients. Resist the urge to layer in tomato or chilli; the canonical version is the one to learn first.
What you need

items.prepNote: two 400 g tins of brown fava beans, drained — or 300 g dried, soaked overnight and cooked
crushed to a paste with a pinch of salt
juice only, plus extra to taste
leaves picked and chopped, to finish
boiled hard, peeled, halved, to serve

content.text: Method
Warm the beans through

If using tinned beans, tip {{broad-beans}} of brown fava beans (drained) into a medium pan. Add a small splash of water, about 100 ml, to keep the beans wet. Set over a medium heat and warm through for ten minutes, stirring occasionally so the bottom does not catch.

If starting from dried beans, soak overnight in plenty of cold water. Drain, cover with fresh water, and simmer for an hour and a half until completely soft. Drain, keeping a cup of the cooking water back, and proceed from here.
Mash to a rough texture
Take the pan off the heat. Mash the beans with a potato masher to a rough texture — half the beans should stay whole, half should be crushed. The dish wants some bite, not a smooth puree.
Season hard

Add {{garlic}} of crushed garlic, {{cumin-ground}} of ground cumin, the juice of {{lemon}} lemon, and {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} of olive oil. Stir through; taste; salt; taste again. Foul medames carries a lot of seasoning. The garlic should be assertive, the lemon should cut through, the cumin should sit underneath.
Plate with the toppings

Spoon the beans into a wide shallow bowl or onto a flat plate. Scatter {{parsley-flat}} of chopped parsley over the top. Add diced fresh tomato if using; sit halved boiled {{eggs}} eggs around the edge. Drizzle another tablespoon of olive oil across the surface. Warm {{pitta-bread}} of pitta in the oven for two minutes and serve alongside.

attrs.heading: Troubleshooting
attrs.intro: Foul is forgiving once the beans are soft. The two failure modes are bland flavour and dry texture.

items.cause: Tinned beans drained and rinsed too thoroughly.
items.fix: Tin liquid carries some of the bean flavour. Drain but do not rinse; if a small amount of liquid clings to the beans that is fine.

items.cause: Not enough liquid added or beans cooked dry.
items.fix: Add another small splash of water or bean cooking liquid while warming through. The finished texture should be loose enough to spoon onto bread without crumbling.
If the cumin tastes raw, toast it for thirty seconds in a dry pan before grinding fresh, or warm it in a tablespoon of the oil over a low flame for a minute before adding to the beans.

items.cause: Dried beans were old or undercooked.
items.fix: Dried fava beans more than a year old never soften properly. Buy fresh stock with a clear pack date and soak for at least twelve hours. If the beans still resist after ninety minutes of simmering, push on to two hours.

content.text: Variations

Egyptian street vendors top foul with tahini stirred through, which turns the dish creamier and richer. A spoon of tahini and a tablespoon of water folded in at the end gives this effect. Some Cairo cooks add a soft-boiled egg directly into the bowl and let it run through the beans.

Levantine versions sometimes add chickpeas alongside the beans for a sweeter mix. Palestinian foul is heavier on the lemon and lighter on the cumin; Yemeni cooks add a green chilli paste called zhug at the table. All recognisable as the same dish, all worth knowing.
Make ahead, freezing, leftovers
Cook the beans the day before; refrigerate covered. Reheat with a splash of water in a small pan, then add lemon, garlic, cumin, and oil fresh. Cooked-and-cooled foul lasts three days in the fridge.

Freezes well for up to two months in portioned containers, before the toppings go on. Defrost overnight in the fridge and reheat slowly. Add the lemon, oil, and parsley after reheating, not before freezing.
Where this dish lives

Foul medames is the daily breakfast of Egypt and one of the most widely eaten dishes of the Arab world. Cairo street vendors set up huge brass pots before dawn and sell it from breakfast through lunch; village kitchens make it the same way. Foul travels well across the Levant and the Gulf, where it appears at the breakfast table in slightly different forms.

The bean itself is older than written cookery. Fava beans have been cultivated in Egypt since the pharaonic period; cooked-bean preparations appear in medieval Arabic culinary texts. The dish you eat for breakfast today is recognisably the dish that fed labourers in twelfth-century Cairo, give or take the lemon and the cumin.
